name: paperbanan-figure-generation
description: Automatically generate publication-ready academic figures and illustrations for AI research papers. Reduces manual illustration work, maintains consistent style, and accelerates paper production—useful for researchers seeking to automate figure creation, maintain visual consistency, and improve presentation quality.
paperTitle: PaperBanana - Automating Academic Illustration for AI Scientists
content:
# PaperBanana: Automating Academic Illustration for AI Scientists

## Core Concept

PaperBanana automates the creation of academic figures for AI research papers by learning from paper content and generating publication-ready illustrations. The system uses multi-modal understanding to identify where figures are needed, determine figure types, and generate appropriate visualizations that enhance paper clarity.

## Architecture Overview

```
Paper Content Input
        ↓
[Figure Need Detector]
   (identifies locations for new figures)
        ↓
[Figure Type Classifier]
   (determines visualization type)
        ↓
[Illustration Generator]
   (creates figure content)
        ↓
[Style Normalizer]
   (ensures consistency)
        ↓
Publication-Ready Figures
```

## Implementation

### 1. Figure Necessity Detection

Analyze paper structure to identify sections lacking visual support.

```python
def detect_figure_locations(paper_text, figure_threshold=0.3):
    """Identify sections needing illustrations."""
    sections = parse_paper_structure(paper_text)

    for section in sections:
        text_length = len(section['content'])
        visual_density = count_existing_figures(section)

        if visual_density < figure_threshold:
            yield {
                'section': section['title'],
                'priority': estimate_clarity_improvement(section),
                'content': section['content']
            }
```

### 2. Figure Type Selection

Determine appropriate visualization types for identified locations.

```python
def select_figure_type(section_content, section_type):
    """Map section to optimal visualization type."""
    type_mapping = {
        'architecture': ['block_diagram', 'flow_chart'],
        'results': ['bar_chart', 'line_plot', 'heatmap'],
        'method': ['process_diagram', 'algorithm_pseudocode'],
        'background': ['taxonomy_diagram', 'concept_map']
    }

    content_keywords = extract_key_terms(section_content)
    return select_best_type(
        type_mapping.get(section_type, []),
        content_keywords
    )
```

### 3. Illustration Generation

Generate figures based on type and content context.

```python
def generate_figure(figure_type, section_content, paper_context):
    """Create figure specification from section content."""

    # Extract data/concepts from text
    extracted_data = parse_section_data(section_content)

    # Generate figure specification
    spec = {
        'type': figure_type,
        'title': generate_figure_title(section_content),
        'data': extracted_data,
        'layout': design_layout(figure_type, extracted_data),
        'style': apply_paper_style(paper_context)
    }

    return render_figure_from_spec(spec)
```

## Practical Guidance

### When to Use
- Research papers with text-heavy sections lacking visual support
- Papers describing complex architectures, methods, or results
- Researchers needing consistent figure style across papers
- Documents where manual illustration is time-consuming

### When NOT to Use
- Papers with extensive custom visualizations already
- Highly domain-specific visualizations requiring expert design
- Figures requiring real experimental data visualization
- Proprietary illustration styles requiring preservation

### Hyperparameters & Configuration

| Parameter | Default | Range | Effect |
|-----------|---------|-------|--------|
| figure_density_target | 0.3 | 0.1-0.7 | % of sections without figures |
| figure_size_preference | medium | small/medium/large | Output figure scale |
| style_consistency | high | low/medium/high | Uniformity across paper |
| generation_quality | standard | fast/standard/high | Rendering detail level |

### Common Pitfalls
- Over-generating figures in already well-illustrated sections
- Misidentifying figure type from ambiguous section content
- Losing domain-specific visualization conventions
- Creating figures with unclear data relationships

## Reference

Key considerations for implementation:
- Figure placement should complement text, not duplicate content
- Style normalization prevents heterogeneous paper appearance
- Iterative feedback improves figure relevance to paper narrative
- Publication guidelines may constrain figure dimensions/format
